Graphs
(cid:73) G = (V , E )
(cid:73) V a set of vertices
(cid:73) E a set of edges; an edge = a non-ordered pair of distinct vertices
V = {0, 1, 2, 3}
E = {{0, 1}, {0, 2}, {1, 2}, {2, 3}}
u = {0, 1} = {1, 0}
0,1 - the ends of u
u is incident with 0 and 1
0 and 1 are adjacent (neighbors)

Graphs
(cid:73) Walk from u to v : u = i0, {i0, i1}, i1, · · · , {ik−1, ik }, ik = v
3, {3,2}, 2, {2,0}, 0, {0,1}, 1, {1,3},3, {3,2},2
(cid:73) Trail: a walk where any two edges are distinct
(cid:73) Path: a walk where any two vertices are distinct
(cid:73) Cycle: closed walk (i0 = ik )
(cid:73) Circuit = a closed walk where any two intermediate vertices are
distinct

The representation of graphs as digraphs
G = (V , E ) =⇒ D(G ) = (V , A)
{i, j} ∈ E =⇒ (i, j), (j, i) ∈ A
(cid:73) the topology is preserved
(cid:73) the adjacency list of i in G = the out (=in) adjacency list of i in D

a[i, j] = if (i, j) ∈ A then 1 else 0
(cid:73) if the digraph is a graph, then a[i, j] is symmetric

The implementation with adjacency matrices
(cid:73) removeVertex()
Procedure removeVertex(a, n, k)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
for j ← 0 to n − 1 do
if (i > k) then
a[i − 1, j] ← a[i, j]
if (j > k) then
a[i, j − 1] ← a[i, j]
n ← n − 1
end
the execution time: O(n2)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
22 / 50

The implementation with adjacency matrices
(cid:73) listOfReachableVertices()
Procedure reﬂTransClosure(a, n, b) // (Warshall, 1962)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
for j ← 0 to n − 1 do
b[i, j] ← a[i, j]
if (i = j) then
b[i, j] ← 1
for k ← 0 to n − 1 do
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
if (b[i, k] = 1) then
for j ← 0 to n − 1 do
if (b[k, j] = 1) then
b[i, j] ← 1
end
the execution time: O(n3)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
23 / 50

Digraphs: systematic exploration
(cid:73) it manages two sets
(cid:73) S = the set of already visited vertices
(cid:73) SB ⊆ S the subset of vertices for which there are chances to ﬁnd
neighbors not visited yet
(cid:73) the adjacency list of i is divided in:
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
28 / 50

Digraphs: systematic exploration
(cid:73) the current step
(cid:73) read a vertex i from SB
(cid:73) extract j from the ”waiting” list of i (if it is nonempty)
(cid:73) if j isn’t in S, then add it to S and to SB
(cid:73) if the ”waiting” list of i is empty, then remove i from SB
(cid:73) initially
(cid:73) S = SB = {i0}
(cid:73) the ”waiting” list of i = the adjacency list of i
(cid:73) termination SB = ∅
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
29 / 50

Digraphs: systematic exploration
Procedure exploration(a, n, i0, S)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
p[i] ← a[i]
SB ← (i0)
visit(i0); S ← (i0)
while (SB (cid:54)= ∅) do
i ← read(SB)
if (p[i] = NULL) then
SB ← SB − {i}
else
j ← p[i] → varf
p[i] ← p[i] → succ
if (j (cid:54)∈ S) then
SB ← SB ∪ {j}
visit(j); S ← S ∪ {j}
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
30 / 50

Systematic exploration: complexity
Theorem
Assuming that the operations over S and SB as well as visit() are
achieved in O(1), the time complexity, in the worst case, of the
exploration algorithm is O(n + m).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
31 / 50

The DFS (Depth First Search) exploration
(cid:73) SB is implemented as a stack
SB ← (i0) ⇔SB ← emptyStack()
push(SB, i0)
i ← read(SB) ⇔i ← top(SB)
SB ← SB − {i} ⇔pop(SB)
SB ← SB ∪ {j} ⇔push(SB, j)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
32 / 50

The DFS exploration: example
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
33 / 50

The BFS (Breadth First Search) exploration
(cid:73) SB is implemented as a queue
SB ← (i0) ⇔SB ← emptyQueue();
insert(SB, i0)
i ← read(SB) ⇔read(SB, i)
SB ← SB − {i} ⇔remove(SB)
SB ← SB ∪ {j} ⇔insert(SB, j)

Lecture 7

Finding the connected components (undirected graphs)
Function ConnectedCompDFS(D)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
color [i] ← 0
k ← 0
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
if (color [i] = 0) then
k ← k + 1
DfsRecConnectedComp(i, k)
return k
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
37 / 50

Finding the connected components (undirected graphs)
Procedure DfsRecConnectedComp(i, k)
begin
color [i] ← k
for (each vertex j in listaDeAdiac(i)) do
if (color [j] = 0) then
DfsRecConnectedComp(j, k)
end

Finding the strongly connected components
Procedure DfsStronglyConnectedComp(D)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
color [i] ← 0
parent[i] ← −1
time ← 0
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
if (color [i] = 0) then
DfsRecStronglyConnectedComp(i)
end

Lecture 7

41 / 50

Finding the strongly connected components
Procedure DfsRecStronglyConnectedComp(i)
begin
time ← time + 1
color [i] ← 1
for (each vertex j in adiacList(i)) do
if (color [j] = 0) then
parent[j] ← i
DfsRecStronglyConnectedComp(j)
time ← time + 1
ﬁnalTime[i] ← time
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
42 / 50

Finding the strongly connected components
Notation: D T = (V , AT ), (i, j) ∈ A ⇔ (j, i) ∈ AT
Procedure StronglyConnectedComp(D)
begin
1. DFSStronglyConnectedComp(D)
2. compute D T
3. DFSStronglyConnectedComp(D T ) but considering in the for main
loop the vertices in descending order of their ﬁnal times of visiting
ﬁnalTime[i]
4. return each tree computed at step 3 as being a distinct strongly
connected component
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
43 / 50

(cid:73) Total: O(n + m)

Applications
The Konigsberg Bridge Problem (1736): starting from one land masses,
walk over each of the seven bridges just once
The land masses: vertices, the bridges: edges
It is possible to choose a vertex, proceed along the edges and return to the
chosen vertex, covering each edge once?
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019

DNA word design
(cid:73) The design of DNA codes that satisfy combinatorial constraints; use
in biomolecular computing to store information, or to manipulate
molecules in chemical libraries
(cid:73) Find the largest set S of strings of length n over the alphabet
{A, C , G , T } s.t.:
(cid:73) GC Content Constraint: each word has 50% symbols from {C , G }
(cid:73) Hamming Distance Constraint: each pair of words, w1 (cid:54)= w2 diﬀer in at
least d positions: H(w1, w2) ≥ d
(cid:73) Reverse Complement Hamming Distance Constraint:
H(R(w1), C (w2)) ≥ d; R(w ): the reversed of word w and C (w ) the
complement of w (C ↔ G , A ↔ T )
FII, UAIC
Lecture 7
DS 2018/2019
48 / 50

(cid:73) a solution: a maximum independent set
